channel.title: Prometheus on Pi Stack
channel.description: Recent content in Prometheus on Pi Stack

item.title: Self-Hosted DNS Cache Monitoring: Unbound vs PowerDNS Recursor vs BIND9 Stats Guide 2026
item.description: If you run a self-hosted DNS resolver, knowing what’s happening inside your cache is just as important as keeping it secure. Cache hit rates, query type distributions, upstream response times — these metrics tell you whether your DNS infrastructure is healthy, efficient, or about to fail.

item.title: mtail vs grok_exporter vs Vector: Self-Hosted Log Parsing Guide 2026
item.description: When your applications generate logs, the raw text is full of useful signals — response times, error rates, request counts, and queue depths. But Prometheus and Grafana can’t read raw log files. You need a log parser to extract metrics from unstructured text and expose them in a format your monitoring stack understands.

item.title: Grafana Mimir vs Thanos vs Cortex: Best Prometheus Long-Term Storage 2026
item.description: Prometheus is the de facto standard for metrics collection, but it was never designed for long-term storage. Its local TSDB keeps data for a limited window before old samples are discarded. For teams that need months or years of historical metrics, three open-source solutions dominate the landscape: Grafana Mimir, Thanos, and Cortex.

item.title: Sloth vs Pyrra vs SLO Generator: Best Self-Hosted SLO Management Tools 2026
item.description: Service Level Objectives (SLOs) are the cornerstone of modern Site Reliability Engineering. They define the reliability target your service promises to users, translate complex metrics into business-readable goals, and drive engineering decisions through error budget tracking. But implementing SLOs by hand — writing PromQL queries, calculating burn rates, configuring multi-window alerts — is error-prone and scales poorly across teams.

item.title: VictoriaMetrics vs Thanos vs Cortex: Best Self-Hosted Metrics Storage 2026
item.description: When running prometheus at scale, you eventually hit the limits of local storage. Metrics accumulate fast, retention policies become critical, and you need horizontal scalability and high availability. That is where distributed metrics storage backends come in.
